displayTitle: 2025-10-15 - Health Committee - Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Health Research Council of New Zealand
topic: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Health Research Council of New Zealand
description: 15 October 2025 | Health Committee Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Health Research Council of New Zealand Health Research Council Lynne Bjarnesen, Chief Financial Officer and Director Corporate Services Dr Tania Pocock, Director Policy and Strategy Professor Lester Levy, Chair Professor Jeroen Douwes, Deputy Chair
descriptionSummary: Review briefing on the 2023/24 annual review of the Health Research Council of New Zealand. Health Research Council. Lynne Bjarnesen, Chief Financial Officer and Director Corporate Services

Good afternoon everybody. Good to have you here.

We have you in here for the annual review of the Health Research Council of New Zealand. I'm Sam Upend, chair of the committee, Dr. Levy, good to have you here. And, uh, profess Levy and everyone else as well. Um, I will open it up for you to make some introductory remarks and then we'll, um, allow members to make some questions. Thank you.

Thank you very much. Chair members of the committee. Excellent. So today I've got with me, uh, professor Rin Do, who's a fellow council member. We've also got Lynn and Tanya Pocock, Dr. Tanya Pocock, who are currently in the interim chief executive role.

So our chief executive after, uh, quite some time as so, Collins, who you would've seen before she resigned, um, in January to go head up the Quality Safety Commission. And currently we have, uh, interim Chief Executive Arrangement. Sorry to interrupt. Can I just get you to move Your mic? Oh, sorry.

Thank you. Is that better? Perfect. Thank you. Uh, so this interim, uh, uh, arrangement will continue until such time as we've appointed the definitive chief executive. Um, in terms of just introductory comments, um, we, we've been making good progress in the Health Research Council.

Uh, you would've seen that there have been announcements, uh, last previously and, and just recently about changes to the Health Research Council and potential changes. Uh, so of course we are, we are adapting to those. And, and these have, um, seen our funding reduced over time.

And so we are adapting the organization in order to deal with, with that. One thing we did have with us today, which if it was appropriate, um, chair for us to distribute for later reading an investment impact report 2005, that probably gives the best perspective of the impact that the Health Research Council is having on the health status of New Zealanders through, um, the investments that we make into health research.

So, we'll, we'll leave those with you. It's not something that, um, is widely publicly available, but it's a very good document to Thank you. Give an insight into what we actually do. Other than that, I think, um, we fine to proceed Chair, Mr. Chair.

Thank you, commissioner and, and your colleagues. Um, I guess I'm really interested to know whether your priorities for research has changed over the last two years, and if so, where are you putting more focus and where less focus and what would the reason for those changes be, if any?

Yeah, well there are some, I'll ask Tanya to talk specifically about this. There are some changes, uh, that we make. These are generally in response to, um, government priorities. Um, and there have been some of those changes. So perhaps I'll ask, uh, Tanya to take you through those.

Thanks, Tanya. Uh, yes. So the priority have been the changes, the changes that we've been have made been very much in relation to the, the leader of expectations from our ministers. Um, so sorry to interrupt, but We are live streaming and I just, it's very, very quiet.

If we can just move up into the Thank You. That's okay. Is that better? Yes, yes, correct. Uh, yes. So the priorities are very much related to the leader of expectations. Um, so there's focus on improving, um, health outcomes focus very much on, uh, how health research is going to impact and make a difference in strengthening the health system and delivery of health services, uh, clinicians and clinician lead research is a key priority as well.

Um, delivering better value across everything that we are doing. Uh, and then the other one is obviously in the kind of economic gain and, um, strengthening economic outcomes and commercialization of research Supplementary. Um, so, and I see that, um, achieving health targets is one of the government's priorities, and that you, that's in the letter of expectation.

Those targets are controversial from our per, from some of our perspective. Um, so can you give me an indication of, given it's kind of a, a refocusing or reprioritization and you do have reduced funding, what are the areas that will now no longer be funded in terms of health research? And in particular, can you tell me about funding relating to equity and to Maori?

Uh, yes, I can. Um, so it, it's really difficult to anticipate in advance of any of any funding what we will be funding less of because so much of what we find is, um, so our research priorities are signaled. Um, and so that gives the research sector an indication of where we would like to invest and what we're looking for.

But it does not mean that anything that is in another area is not going to get through on basis of Just a final one, if I may. Thank you. Does the letter of expectation now mean that there will be more focus on that and potentially less focus on screening for various health things? How are we going to ensure that screening remains a priority for health research, given that that is not con concluded in the government targets? And there's still significant investment in that. And so What's the difference now on the level of investment? Excellent. Um, can you tell us what the six contestable funding rounds were available to apply for in the 20 23 20 24 year? The six contestable rounds? Yes.

I'd have to come back for each of those specific, Do you have a rough indication? The rough indication? Well, the, the health research council primary responsibility is to invest into health research in New Zealand. However, I know that this is the new area, so I know there's, there's not much detail. Um, there's no baseline baseline value we can compare or, or, or the new measurement. Can you give a bit more detail like how you guys could measure the success for this outcome? Yeah, so I, I lost Tanya to talk to the detail about that, but just in principle, we have, uh, in our more recent RO rounds, we've had a specific call around artificial intelligence, which is the first one for us, specifically orientated to that.

But that doesn't preclude, um, investment opportunities in our other rounds. Uh, for artificial intelligence, obviously it's a fast moving field and there's more experience and opportunity there. And, and as part of that, uh, one of our new counseling members who was appointed has got specific expertise in the world of artificial intelligence.

But you may want to comment a bit further about the actual outcome of the grants. Uh, I can give you a little bit more detail about the investments that we've made. So, um, with the most recent, um, request for proposals that went out, we council agreed to fund 10 of the contracts for 4.6 million. Thank you. How do we know that Maori are going to have an equitable share of the funding or the necessary share of the funding research? Um, it's, uh, it has averaged about 13% over the past five years, but in 2024, um, it was 21.5%, um, of the grants that were, um, Great, thank you. So they, they take a, they take essentially a year from when they opened when we announced results. Um, Well, the previous round, the delays in that, so there was, um, a short delay in terms of having the meeting. So I assume one of those changes is the fact that it's not going out to international peer review. How are you going to assume that we're gonna be internationally competitive without that step? Uh, well, we, it's based on the fact that we, um, we we're actually following international practice, um, and there has been quite a bit of analysis done internationally and we replicated that internally as well.

Um, it's not that there isn't peer review, um, there is definitely peer review. What we found was that we have, we have the most peer review steps in the, in any system in the world. We have four. Um, and there was no difference in the ranking or the position of a grant for the first two steps, which are the OEOI steps when we get the application to assess.

And then there, there was very, very little not statistically significant difference between the ranking and what was funded between the two levels of peer review, which are the international and the, um, and the committee peer review. So that was what informed, um, that change. So just so, so you, it is just going, you're skipping the international step, but leaving the committee step in in, in place, yes.

So the members would make recommendations, but specific members and the secretariat then invites them, and that's how it's being decided. But, um, obviously dependent on the type of applications that we receive, uh, we, we would focus on specific, um, expertise areas.

Uh, essentially our strategic plan is our statement of intent. Yes. So well, um, the, so Collins, uh, been with us about five and a half years.
